figures.caption: Figure 1: Standard video generation vs. PILA. Standard generators may produce physically implausible dynamics. PILA introduces a physics-structured latent adapter with routed experts and flow correction to improve physical plausibility.

Figure 2: Pipeline of PILA. The method proceeds through three stages: (1) operational physical interface encoding and label-prior expert routing (Sec. 3.1); (2) physics-structured operator refinement of category-specific field-proxy representations (Sec. 3.2); and (3) physics-to-latent flow alignment through a lightweight correction to the frozen flow-matching vector field (Sec. 3.3).
Table 1: Category-specific operational residual constraints. For each expert, we list the field proxies and representative residual terms Ck,i used during training.
Figure 3: Training data distribution across physical categories. We relabel WISA-80K into eight physical categories, grouped into four material categories and four phenomenon categories.

Table 2: Quantitative comparison on VideoPhy [42] dataset. Joint and Rule are evaluated using VideoPhy-2 [43]. Dynamic, Mechanics, Material, Motion are evaluated using VBench-2.0 [44]. Imaging Quality (Imaging) is evaluated using VBench [45]. The † row transfers the Wan 2.1-trained adapter to Wan 2.2-14B without 14B-specific training. Bold: Best. Underline: Second Best.

Table 3: Quantitative comparison with baselines evaluated on PhyGenBench [46] dataset. Mechanics, Optics, Thermal, Material and Average are evaluated using PhyGenBench [46]. Motion is evaluated using VBench-2.0 [44]. Imaging Quality (Imaging) is evaluated using VBench [45].
Figure 4: Qualitative comparison with baselines. PILA better captures physically grounded interactions and state changes across diverse phenomena.
Table 4: Ablation study on Wan 2.1-1.3B. All variants use the same backbone and training budget.
Figure 7: Visualization of routed expert activations. Expert maps show that PILA applies categoryspecific updates to physically active regions.
Table 5: AFE field constructors. Each constructor produces an operational field proxy that is repacked into the fixed attribute-bank contract.

Figure 8: Prompt-refinement template used before LPMER routing. The template rewrites a raw input prompt into a concise physics-oriented description that emphasizes objects, motion, interactions, material cues, and state changes. This refined description is used as cleaner prompt evidence for the subsequent category-labeling and label-prior routing steps.

Figure 9: LLM-based category-label estimation template for LPMER. Given the refined physicsoriented description, the template asks the LLM to select the relevant physical categories from the eight-category expert set P . The resulting multi-label set Y is used as label-prior evidence for routing.

Table 10: Operational residual-family ablations on Wan 2.1-1.3B. Each row removes one residual subfamily, using the grouping clarified in Tables 6–7, while keeping the same backbone and evaluation protocol.

Figure 10: Effect of the denoising-step schedule. Gains are computed relative to the no-adapter baseline in Table 13. Expanding the sigma-gated correction window improves physical average and motion scores while preserving imaging quality.

Table 13: Denoising-step sensitivity of the physics adapter on Wan 2.1-1.3B. We vary the denoisingstep range in which sigma-gated physical corrections are applied while keeping the same backbone and evaluation protocol.
Figure 11: Qualitative comparison in the 1.3B–5B setting. PILA produces more physically plausible motion and interactions under the same prompts.
Figure 12: Qualitative comparison in the high-capacity setting. PILA produces more plausible physical evolution under the same prompts.
Figure 13: Additional PILA generations across physical categories. Each row shows sampled frames from one physical category in our eight-category taxonomy.
